turns.content: I'm trying to understand how to split a stream of sensor readings into two separate vectors, one for values above a threshold and another for the rest. I've heard you can use an iterator method for this instead of collecting everything first.

You are looking at the partition method on iterators which takes a predicate closure and yields two iterators: one containing elements where your predicate returns true, and another for false. This avoids materializing the full collection in memory before splitting it. If you want to group adjacent identical readings instead of filtering by value, you would use the group_by method which groups consecutive equal items into tuples of (key, iterator).

That makes sense for partitioning, but I'm running into an edge case where my closure returns true only on even indices, and I need to know exactly how many elements went into each bucket without storing all of them. Can you explain the difference between using partition with a simple predicate versus using group_by when my data has runs of identical values?

Partition simply forwards every element that matches your closure to one output stream and ignores it for the other, so if your predicate depends on index you cannot use it directly unless you wrap the iterator in something like a Peekable or collect into a Vec first. Group_by is specifically designed to group adjacent equal elements into a vector of (key, iterator) tuples where each key repeats until the next distinct value appears; this is useful when you want to compress consecutive duplicates rather than filter by condition.
I'm struggling with Rust iterators, specifically the difference between partition and group_by. I know I've used vec and iter before, but how do these actually fit into that?

The key distinction is that partition splits a stream into two separate collections based on a predicate, while group_by clusters adjacent identical elements. When you use partition with an impl IntoIterator trait, it creates vecs where one holds items matching the condition and the other holds those that don't. In contrast, group_by groups consecutive duplicates together, which is useful for counting runs of values without needing to sort first.
So I can just call these methods directly on an iterator? Like if I have a vec of strings, I could split them by length?

Exactly. If you define a predicate that checks the byte length of each string and apply partition, it will yield two vecs: one with short names and another with long ones. However, group_by won't do this because it only groups adjacent equal values; if your input isn't sorted by length, group_by will just create a single group containing all strings since they are never identical.
Ah, I see. But what if I want to count how many times each character appears in a sentence?

That's an excellent use case for group_by. You can iterate through the characters, and since you're grouping adjacent identical elements, every run of 'a's will be collected into one vec. Then you map over those groups to count their length, effectively tallying each unique character sequence.
That makes sense now. Can I still use partition for counting instead?

Not really, because partition doesn't group adjacent duplicates together; it only separates items based on a boolean condition. For your character count scenario, you'd need to ensure the stream is sorted by character value first if using partition logic, which defeats the purpose of grouping nearby runs.
